narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your undergraduate studies and preparing for the LSAT. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 30, placement in the 3rd decile for your Year 1 school grades and the 2nd decile for Year 3, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing at 65.1%—a threshold that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed on the bar. The most influential factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile placement and signaled weaker performance relative to peers, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, also below average at the 3rd decile compared to the usual 5th or 6th. These early and later academic rankings stood out as the strongest drivers, suggesting to the model that sustaining high performance might be challenging for you compared to most applicants. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, though below the average of around 37, still nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, as did your undergraduate GPA—which, at 2.80 versus the typical 3.21, offered some counterbalance but not enough—and your full-time enrollment plans, which align with the vast majority of successful students. Your middle family income was a minor factor leaning toward failure but paled in comparison to the academic deciles' impact. Overall, your situation differs from typical admitted students, who tend to rank higher in school deciles and score stronger on key tests, making their bar pass predictions more favorable. While this decision is disappointing, especially given the unique barriers Black women like yourself often face in competitive fields, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic decile rankings through targeted study habits or additional coursework, retake the LSAT to aim above the average, and consider part-time options or gap-year experiences that demonstrate growth. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these core areas—keep pushing forward, and we're rooting for your success.
